did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a neural network (NN) based on the pre-trained EfficientNet B7 architecture, implemented in Python with TensorFlow 2.0, to classify four types of benign melanocytic skin tumors (blue nevus, complex nevus, dermal nevus, and Spitz-nevus) from dermatoscopic images in pediatric patients. The AI model achieved 83% accuracy and was integrated into a mobile app for diagnostic assistance.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' and presents original experimental work. It details the design, training, and validation of a neural network (EfficientNet B7) on a specific dataset of 600 pediatric patients (augmented to 1800 images) to diagnose benign skin tumors. The paper includes original methods, results (83% accuracy, sensitivity/specificity metrics), and the integration of the AI model into a mobile app, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Artificial Intelligence (Neural Network) in the Diagnosis of Benign Skin Tumors in Pediatric Patients
journal: European Journal of Clinical and Biomedical Sciences
brief_summary: This study developed and evaluated a neural network to classify four types of benign melanocytic skin tumors (blue nevus, complex nevus, dermal nevus, and Spitz-nevus) in pediatric patients using dermatoscopic images. The model, based on the pre-trained EfficientNet B7 architecture and implemented in TensorFlow 2.0, was trained on a dataset of 1800 images derived from 600 patients through augmentation techniques. The AI system achieved an overall accuracy of 83% on a test set of 56 images, with sensitivity ranging from 73% to 100% and specificity from 82% to 98% across the tumor types. The diagnostic tool was integrated into a mobile application named "KIDS NEVI" to facilitate clinical decision-making. The results demonstrate that AI can serve as a high-potential auxiliary method for the rapid and correct diagnosis of benign skin tumors in children and adolescents.

all_task_types.target: Four types of benign melanocytic skin tumors (blue nevus, complex nevus, dermal nevus, and Spitz-nevus)
all_task_types.details: The neural network performs supervised learning to classify dermatoscopic images into discrete histological categories. The task is a multiclass classification problem with four target classes. The model achieved 83% accuracy on a test set of 56 images. Metrics reported include precision, recall (sensitivity), specificity, F1-score, and AUC for each class.

datasets_used.name: Pediatric Benign Skin Tumors Dataset (N. N. Petrov National Medical Research Center of Oncology)
datasets_used.description: A private, single-center dataset of dermatoscopic images collected from pediatric and adolescent patients (aged 2-17 years) observed at the N. N. Petrov National Medical Research Center of Oncology between 2016 and 2022. The original cohort consisted of 600 patients, with histological verification performed on a subset of 65 cases. The dataset was augmented to 1800 images for training. Patients with pyogenic granuloma and melanoma were excluded from the final analysis.

use.training: Used for supervised learning of the EfficientNet B7 neural network. The original 600 images were augmented (reflected, enlarged, lighting/contrast changes) to 1800 images to improve generalization.
use.testing: Used to assess model accuracy (83%), sensitivity, specificity, and other metrics. The test set consisted of 56 dermatoscopic images with histological verification.
use.other: Excluded classes (Pyogenic granuloma, Melanoma) were present in the initial cohort but removed from the study due to different tumorogenesis or poor photographic material.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To visualize and calculate diagnostic errors, matches, and metrics (precision, recall, specificity, F1-score) by comparing predicted values against actual histologic diagnoses.

statistical_methods_used.method: Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curves and Area Under Curve (AUC)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To analyze the association between technique and morphology and to evaluate the diagnostic performance of the model for each tumor class.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Diagnostic performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score, and AUC, without statistical inference.

diagnostic_errors.description: Maximum diagnostic error found in identification of complex nevus. In five cases, the lesion was identified as a dermal nevus.
diagnostic_errors.total_errors: Single numbers outside the main diagonal in confusion matrix confirm high information content; specific error counts per class not explicitly listed beyond the complex nevus example.

conclusions: The AI model, based on the pre-trained EfficientNet B7 architecture, achieved an overall accuracy of 83% in classifying four types of benign melanocytic skin tumors (blue nevus, complex nevus, dermal nevus, and Spitz-nevus) in pediatric patients.
Image augmentation was critical for model performance; without augmentation, the model's accuracy did not exceed 70%, whereas with augmentation, it reached 83%.
The AI system demonstrated high sensitivity for blue nevus (100%) and dermal nevus (93%), and moderate sensitivity for complex nevus (73%) and Spitz-nevus (75%).
The maximum diagnostic error occurred in the identification of complex nevi, which were misclassified as dermal nevi in five cases.
The AI diagnostic tool was successfully integrated into a mobile application named "KIDS NEVI" for Android devices.
The authors conclude that AI serves as a high-potential auxiliary method for the rapid and correct diagnosis of benign skin tumors in children and adolescents.
The combination of dermatoscopic analysis and the mobile app provides a "double control" mechanism to facilitate quick clinical diagnosis and determine treatment tactics.
important_remarks: The study relies on a very small test set of only 56 dermatoscopic images with histological verification, which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the reported 83% accuracy.

The training dataset was artificially expanded from 600 to 1800 images via augmentation (reflection, enlargement, lighting/contrast changes); the paper notes that accuracy did not exceed 70% without augmentation, indicating high dependence on this technique.

The dataset is heavily imbalanced, with rare classes such as blue nevus (n=2) and Spitz-nevus (n=4) having very few histologically verified samples, potentially biasing the model's performance metrics for these classes.

The study is limited to a single-center cohort (N. N. Petrov National Medical Research Center of Oncology) in Russia, raising concerns about external validation and potential population or device-specific bias.

Patients with pyogenic granuloma and melanoma were excluded from the final analysis due to different tumorogenesis or poor photographic material, limiting the model's applicability to differential diagnosis against these common mimics.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals for the diagnostic metrics, relying solely on descriptive statistics.

The AI model was integrated into a mobile app ('KIDS NEVI') for Android, but no clinical workflow validation or user study regarding the app's practical utility or safety in a real-world pediatric setting is provided.
